name: Matacás Viewpoint Trail
name_original: Mirador de Matacás – circular desde Castro Caldelas – Cañón del Sil
description: Hike the demanding 10.0-mile Mirador de Matacas circular trail from Castro Caldelas for stunning Sil Canyon views and terraced vineyards.
region_intro: Hiking around Castro Caldelas offers diverse terrain characterized by river valleys, canyons, and elevated viewpoints. The region features paths along the Mao River, providing access to boardwalks and natural landscapes. Trails often lead through areas with historical significance, including routes near Castro Caldelas Castle. The landscape provides varied elevation changes suitable for different hiking experiences. Best hiking trails around Castro Caldelas The most popular hiking route is Río Mao Canyon Route, a 3.2 miles (5.1 km) trail that takes 1 hour 45 minutes to complete. This easy route follows the river through a scenic canyon. Another top favourite among local hikers is Matacás Viewpoint Trail, a difficult 10.0 miles (16.1 km) path. This trail offers significant elevation gain leading to panoramic views. Local hikers also love the Castro Caldelas Castle loop from O Castro de Caldelas, a 3.4 miles (5.4 km) trail leading through historical areas, often completed in about 1 hour 36 minutes. Hiking around Castro Caldelas is defined by river valleys, canyon landscapes, and historical sites. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy walks to challenging hikes for experienced hikers. The routes in Castro Caldelas are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 130 reviews. More than 1000 hikers have used komoot to explore Castro Caldelas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Castro Caldelas?
A: There are over 25 hiking routes in the Castro Caldelas region, offering a diverse range of experiences. These include 14 easy trails, 9 moderate options, and 4 more challenging routes for experienced hikers.

Q: Are there any easy, family-friendly hikes in Castro Caldelas?
A: Yes, Castro Caldelas offers several easy trails suitable for families. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e976182932/rio-mao-canyon-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Mao Canyon Route&lt;/a&gt; is a popular choice, an easy 5.1 km (3.2 miles) path that follows the river through a scenic canyon. Another great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10460311" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mao River Boardwalk loop from Ruta A fabrica da Luz&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 3.7 km (2.3 miles) and features accessible boardwalks.

Q: Where can I find challenging hikes for experienced hikers in the area?
A: For experienced hikers seeking a challenge, the region offers several difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984231586/matacas-viewpoint-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Matacás Viewpoint Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 16.1 km (10.0 miles) path with significant elevation gain, leading to panoramic views. Another difficult option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6951584" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mao River Boardwalk – Conceliñas Medieval Bridge loop from Ruta A fabrica da Luz&lt;/a&gt;, also around 16.1 km (10.0 miles).

Q: Are there any circular walks around Castro Caldelas?
A: Yes, many trails in Castro Caldelas are designed as circular routes, allowing you to start and end at the same point. A notable example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14935632" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Caldelas Castle loop from O Castro de Caldelas&lt;/a&gt;, a 5.4 km (3.4 miles) trail that explores historical areas around the castle.

Q: What kind of natural attractions or viewpoints can I expect to see on the trails?
A: The hiking trails around Castro Caldelas are rich in natural beauty and offer stunning viewpoints. You can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3852882" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mao River Boardwalk&lt;/a&gt;, discover impressive gorges, and visit several scenic overlooks. Highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4576799" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Balcones de Madrid Viewpoint (Os Torgás Viewpoint)&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7239263" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;As Fontiñas Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5303453" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pena de Matacás viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, all offering expansive views of the surrounding landscape.

Q: Are there historical sites or landmarks to explore while hiking?
A: Absolutely. The region is dotted with historical sites. Beyond the prominent Castro Caldelas Castle, you can visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4624267" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Vítor de Barxacova Chapel and Necropolis&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a glimpse into the area's past. Many trails weave through areas with significant cultural heritage.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Castro Caldelas?
A: The best time to hike in Castro Caldelas is generally during spring (April-June) and autumn (September-October) when the weather is mild and the natural scenery is vibrant. Spring brings blooming flora, while autumn offers spectacular fall colors, especially along the river valleys and chestnut trails. Summer can be warm, and winter, while quieter, may have colder temperatures and occasional rain.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Castro Caldelas?
A: The hiking routes in Castro Caldelas are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average rating of 4.7 stars from over 130 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the diverse terrain, the beauty of the river valleys and canyons, and the well-maintained boardwalks. Many appreciate the blend of natural landscapes with historical elements.

Q: Are there any trails that follow rivers or have boardwalks?
A: Yes, the Mao River is a central feature of many trails in the region. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3852882" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mao River Boardwalk&lt;/a&gt; is a popular attraction, providing an accessible way to experience the river valley. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e976182932/rio-mao-canyon-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Mao Canyon Route&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10460311" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mao River Boardwalk loop from Ruta A fabrica da Luz&lt;/a&gt; incorporate these scenic river paths and boardwalks.

Q: Are there any specific trails known for their natural monuments or unique flora?
A: The region is home to several natural monuments. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6125209" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chestnut Trail in Parada de Sil&lt;/a&gt; is particularly notable for its ancient chestnut trees, offering a unique natural experience. The diverse landscape also includes the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6560211" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lor River&lt;/a&gt;, contributing to the rich biodiversity of the area.

Q: What should I wear for hiking in Castro Caldelas during autumn?
A: For autumn hiking in Castro Caldelas, it's advisable to dress in layers. Temperatures can vary, so bring a waterproof and windproof outer shell, a warm mid-layer (like a fleece), and moisture-wicking base layers. Sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support are essential, especially on trails with varied terrain. Don't forget a hat, gloves, and plenty of water.

Q: Is parking available at the trailheads in Castro Caldelas?
A: While specific parking details vary by trailhead, many popular hiking routes around Castro Caldelas, especially those starting near villages or key attractions, offer designated parking areas. It's always recommended to check the specific route details on komoot for information on parking availability and accessibility before you set out.
tips:
More than 500 meters above the Peñas de Matacás there is a spectacular viewpoint with views of the Sil Canyon. This section of the River Sil acts as a natural border between the provinces of Lugo and Ourense.
From this fantastic balcony over the void you can see the piers of Doade, in Sober and Castro Caldelas.

Magnificent medieval castle located in the town of Castro Caldelas (Ourense). During the Middle Ages it was the most important fortress in the Ribeira Sacra and played a prominent role. The castle was also home to military legions, armies, senior officers and orders of warrior monks.

faqs.question: How difficult is the Mirador de Matacas circular hike?
faqs.answer: This trail is rated as difficult. It covers over 16 km with significant elevation changes (over 600m up), including a very steep initial descent from Castro Caldelas and a demanding climb back towards the end. A good physical condition and sure-footedness are essential.

faqs.question: Where does the trail start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The trail typically starts and ends in the charming village of Castro Caldelas. You should find parking available within the village, which also serves as an excellent base with amenities before and after your hike.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The scenery is particularly spectacular in autumn, when the terraced vineyards of the Ribeira Sacra transform into vibrant reddish and golden hues. Spring also offers pleasant temperatures and lush landscapes, making it a great time to visit.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for hiking in Ribeira Sacra?
faqs.answer: No, there are generally no specific permits or entrance fees required to hike this trail or access the natural areas within the Ribeira Sacra. However, always respect local regulations and private property.

faqs.question: Is this trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the trail itself is generally accessible, it's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially given the varied terrain, potential wildlife, and presence of other hikers. Ensure your dog is fit enough for a difficult, long hike with steep sections.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the Mirador de Matacas trail?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite varied, consisting of approximately 50% dirt paths, 35% tracks, and 15% asphalt. You'll traverse lush chestnut groves, terraced vineyards, and narrow paths around the rocky outcrops of 'As Penas de Matacás'.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: Key highlights include the breathtaking panoramic views from the Pena de Matacás viewpoint overlooking the Sil Canyon, the historic Castro Caldelas Castle, and the iconic terraced vineyards of the Ribeira Sacra. You'll also pass through traditional black slate villages and serene forests.

faqs.question: Does this trail connect with other hiking paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route, also known as PR-G 237, incorporates sections of several other official trails. You'll find yourself on parts of the Sendeiro do Miradoiro de Matacás, Roteiros de Matacás, and Sendeiro dos Bosques Máxicos do Castro de Caldelas, among others.

faqs.question: What should I be aware of regarding the steep sections of the trail?
faqs.answer: Be prepared for a very steep descent at the beginning from Castro Caldelas, which can be challenging for knees. The final section also involves a demanding climb back up. Consider using trekking poles for stability and to ease the strain on descents and ascents.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or water sources along the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail itself is primarily natural, so facilities are limited once you leave Castro Caldelas. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks for the entire duration of the hike. Castro Caldelas offers cafes and other amenities before and after your adventure.

faqs.question: What makes the vineyards along this route special?
faqs.answer: The vineyards you'll see are a prime example of 'heroic viticulture.' They are meticulously cultivated on steep, terraced slopes, a testament to centuries of human effort to grow mencía grapes in challenging terrain. The visual contrast with the wilder slopes of the Sil Canyon is striking.
